title: Think OS: A Brief Introduction to Operating Systems
author: Allen B. Downey
target_audience: Undergraduate computer science students taking a first course on operating systems, with prior programming experience in C or Python.
prerequisites_inferred: Working knowledge of the C programming language
Familiarity with basic data structures and pointers
Comfort with a Unix-like command line
Introductory computer architecture (what a CPU, register, and memory address are)
learning_outcomes_inferred: Explain how compilation, linking, and loading turn source code into a running process
Describe the abstractions an operating system provides for processes, memory, and files
Reason about virtual memory, address translation, and paging
Understand caching, locality, and the memory hierarchy
Use and reason about concurrency primitives — threads, mutexes, condition variables, and semaphores
Analyze simple OS-level C programs that exercise these abstractions
draft_notes: Version 0.7.4 — incomplete draft. The preface notes the book is a work in progress; chapters may change before the 1.0 release.
subject: Operating Systems
description: A concise undergraduate introduction to operating systems concepts using C examples, covering processes, virtual memory, file systems, memory management, caching, and concurrency.
